Atomic-Scale Phase Composition through Multivariate
Statistical Analysis of Atom Probe Tomography Data
Michael R. Keenan, 1,* Vincent S. Smentkowski, 2 Robert M. Ulfig, 3 Edward Oltman, 3
David J. Larson, 3 and Thomas F . Kelly 3
18346 Roney Road, Wolcott, NY 14590, USA
2General Electric Global Research Center, Niskayuna, NY 12309, USA
3CAMECA Instruments, Inc., 5500 Nobel Drive, Madison, WI 53711, USA
Abstract: W e demonstrate for the first time that multivariate statistical analysis techniques can be applied to
atom probe tomography data to estimate the chemical composition of a sample at the full spatial resolution of
the atom probe in three dimensions. Whereas the raw atom probe data provide the specific identity of an
atom at a precise location, the multivariate results can be interpreted in terms of the probabilities that an atom
representing a particular chemical phase is situated there. When aggregated to the size scale of a single atom
~;0.2 nm!, atom probe spectral-image datasets are huge and extremely sparse. In fact, the average spectrum will
have somewhat less than one total count per spectrum due to imperfect detection efficiency. These conditions,
under which the variance in the data is completely dominated by counting noise, test the limits of multivariate
analysis, and an extensive discussion of how to extract the chemical information is presented. Efficient
numerical approaches to performing principal component analysis ~PCA! on these datasets, which may number
hundreds of millions of individual spectra, are put forward, and it is shown that PCA can be computed in a few
seconds on a typical laptop computer.
Key words: atom probe tomography ~APT!, multivariate statistical analysis ~MVSA!, PCA
INTRODUCTION
Multivariate statistical analysis ~MVSA! has proven to be an
effective approach for extracting the chemical information
from large spectral-image datasets in three spatial dimen-
sions. Examples include tomographic spectral imaging with
energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy ~EDXS!~ Kotula et al.,
2006!, and time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry
~TOF-SIMS!~ Smentkowski et al., 2007 !. In these cases,
a complete spectrum is acquired at each point, or voxel,
in a regular three-dimensional ~3D! array yielding an
abundance of data that can be cumbersome to analyze.
The assumption underlying MVSA is that a multivariate
observation—namely, an n-channel spectrum—is a linear
combination of a small number of hidden, or latent, vari-
ables. The goal of MVSA is to discover the latent variables,
which presumably describe the fundamental spectral char-
acteristics of the substances that make up the sample. If
there are p such latent variables with p
/H11021/H11021n,M V S Aa c c o m -
plishes a large reduction in the size and dimensionality of
the dataset. The presence of correlations among measured
variables is essential to the operation of MVSA. It is the
redundant information provided by correlated variables
that is discarded as the dataset is compressed by MVSA. For
a mass spectrometric detection scheme, correlated variables
might include, for example, multiple isotopes of a given
element or several elements that co-vary in a regular way as
in an alloy.
Implicit in the foregoing discussion is the notion that
several variables are measured simultaneously at each spa-
tial location, that is, that the observation is truly multivari-
ate. Atom probe tomography ~APT! data, on the other
hand, would seem to be fundamentally univariate in nature.
Since single atoms are detected one at a time, it is not
obvious how the interspecies correlations that MVSA ex-
ploits are captured in the data at the atomic level. Nonethe-
less, there has been recent interest in applying multivariate
techniques to APT data ~Keenan et al., 2010; Parish et al.,
2010; Parish & Miller, 2010 !. The common approach in the
cited works was to partition the sampled volume into a
large number of volume elements and to combine all of the
ions detected in each individual element into a single mass
spectrum. Typically, the volume elements were on the order
of 1 nm
3 and contained several to a few tens of detected
ions. Likewise, masses were binned to account for the fact
that the mass measurements have some imprecision. MVSA
was then performed on the aggregated data. Both groups
employed the same approach ~Keenan, 2009; Smentkowski
et al., 2009 ! whereby principal component analysis ~PCA!
was used to compress the data and extract the chemical
information, and the principal components were, option-
ally, post-processed to improve interpretability.
MVSA of APT data aggregated in this manner proved
successful, but at the cost of losing the high spatial resolu-
tion characteristic of atom probes. For a nominal lateral
resolution of 0.2 nm, binning to 1 nm
3 implies a factor of
125 loss of resolution on a volumetric basis. In this article
we will use simple arguments and simulations to suggest

that MVSA, in fact, can be successfully applied to APT data
aggregated to the native spatial resolution of the instru-
ment. Thus, we can gain the advantages of MVSA, namely,
discovering relationships among elements, while retaining
full spatial resolution. The arguments turn on the insight
that while the atom probe only detects single ions, those can
be considered random samples drawn from a local neigh-
borhood defined by the spatial resolution of the instru-
ment. When constructed at the atomic scale, the multivariate
model has a natural interpre tation. Rather than predicting
which specific atom is situated at a particular location, the
model predicts the probabilities that atoms of any type are
located there. In essence, the chemical-phase composition at
the atomic scale is resolved. These principles will be further
illustrated by analyzing an APT dataset obtained from a
two-phase region of a nickel-base superalloy sample using
PCA.
It should be noted that analyzing data binned to the
size of single atoms poses significant computational chal-
lenges. The number of voxels can easily reach tens to hun-
dreds of millions. Despite the high detection efficiency of
atom probes ~up to ;60%!, less than one ion will be
observed, on average, as arising from any single voxel.
Consequently, the matrices representing APT mass spectral
datasets are extremely sparse, a fact that must be exploited
to make the MVSA calculations tractable. Further efficien-
cies can be achieved by recalling that finding correlations
among variables is the central function of MVSA. Since the
observation of a single ion in isolation provides no informa-
tion about interatom correlations, we can restrict our atten-
tion to those mass spectra that contain two or more total
counts. This will typically be a tiny fraction of the total
number of spectra ~voxels!, yet still a large number in
absolute terms. With these considerations in mind, we will
discuss a computational approach using standard mathemat-
ical tools ~e.g., MATLAB, http://www.mathworks.com! that
enables the MVSA calculations to be performed on comput-
ers with modest capabilities. Using these techniques, PCA of
the superalloy dataset below was accomplished in a few
seconds on a standard laptop computer.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
The APT dataset employed in this work was obtained from
a sample of a nickel-base superalloy. Non-site-specific APT
specimens were prepared using the matchstick method and
electropolishing in 10% perchloric acid in acetic acid. Analy-
ses were obtained on a Cameca LEAP 3000X HR in voltage-
pulsed mode. This instrument has a detection efficiency of
37% of the ions independent of ion type. Atom positioning
precision is better than 1 nm in all locations and can be as
high as 0.06 nm in the longitudinal direction ~Z axis! of the
data ~Gault et al., 2010!.A no v e r v i e wo fa t o mp r o b et o m o g -
raphy can be found in Kelly and Miller ~2007!.
Figure 1a shows a 3D rendering of the total ion count
for this sample, which was obtained by binning the detected
ions into 1 nm
3 cubes. The ions included here were limited
to those having a mass-to-charge-state ratio of 100 Da or
less. The sample extends 171 nm in the axial dimension,
and at its maximum lateral extent, the sample has a diam-
eter of approximately 144 nm. A total of more than 55
million ions were detected. T wo regions of interest ~ROIs!
were selected for further study. A two-phase region shown
in Figure 1b was used to test the multivariate approach on a
sample having heterogeneous composition. This ROI com-
prises the top 39 nm of the sample, has a lateral extent of
100 nm at its base, and contains approximately 7.4 million
detected ions. For the multivariate analysis, a spectrum at
each voxel was constructed by binning all ions at a given
location into bins of 0.5 Da width centered on unit and
half-unit mass-to-charge-state ratio. The second ROI shown
in Figure 1c is a rectangular prism measuring 80 /H1100380 /H1100356
nm
3 and was used to evaluate the statistical properties of
APT data for a relatively homogeneous material. 12.5 mil-
lion detected ions were contained in this ROI.
Simple simulations that were performed were based on
the physical properties of pure nickel. The relevant proper-
ties include the relative abundances of the five naturally
occurring isotopes of pure nickel ~Rosman & T aylor, 1998!,
and the face-centered-cubic ~fcc! crystal structure of nickel
with a lattice parameter of 0.352 nm ~Barrett & Massalski,
1980!. It was assumed in all simulations that the probability
of detecting any given ion was 37%, regardless of the type of
ion or its location. Under these conditions, the data are
expected to follow the multinomial distribution ~Larsen &
Marx, 1986! because six possible outcomes ~an ion is one of
the five isotopes, or no ion is detected !, each with a known
probability of occurring, span the observation space when
looking at all atom positions one at a time. T o perform the
simulations, a simple multinomial random number gener-
ator was developed that was based on random numbers
uniformly distributed on the interval from zero to one.
Since the probabilities of the six events sum to one, all
possible outcomes can be mapped to nonoverlapping sub-
intervals on @01 #, where lengths of the subintervals are
equal to the respective probabilities of occurring. An “ob-

when binned to 1 nm in each dimension. b: Region of interest is a
39 nm thick two-phase zone. c: A volume, representing a single

erties of APT data.

Multivariate Statistical Analysis

that the chemistry and physics that underlie a spectral
dataset can be described by a linear additive model. Given a
sample composed of p pure chemical components, ele-
ments, or mixtures of elements that vary in the same way
with each possessing a character istic spectrum, the spec-
trum at any location is the sum of the pure-component
spectra weighted by their respective abundances at that
location. In the absence of noise,

It should be mentioned that in Keenan and Kotula
~2004! scaling was also applied in the spatial domain. This is
important in the case of EDXS and TOF-SIMS, where
different materials in a sample frequently have vastly differ-
ent spectral intensities. This is not expected to be the case in
APT because the detection efficiency is independent of the
type of atom, and atom densities typically do not differ
widely with material. Given the poor signal-to-noise of
binned APT data, including a huge number of parameters
~one per voxel! to model noise in the spatial domain would
seem unwarranted and, in our experience, has deleterious
effects on the derived components.
The scaling method outlined in the previous paragraph

sample. All calculations were performed with MATLAB,

ing author. The raw mass spectral data are presented to the
user after reconstruction in the form of a binary file as a list
of position-tagged masses ~*.pos file!. This is fundamentally
a sparse data storage format, and the data were converted

RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
Noise Characteristics of APT Data
The nickel-base superalloy ROI shown in Figure 1c provides
an opportunity to investigate the noise characteristics of
spatially binned APT data. Since this volume contains an
essentially homogeneous material, it can be modeled in a
simple intuitive way without resorting to multivariate tech-
niques. Specifically, all voxels are assumed to have the same
underlying composition whose mass spectrum is given by
the mean spectrum over the entire dataset. In terms of the
linear model of equation ~1!, the noise-free data can be
modeled as

which seek to maximize the amount of variance that the
most significant components explain, will find it more
profitable to uncover structure in the noise than to discover
the underlying chemistry of the sample. As noted in the
previous section, the key to achieving good results with
MVSA is to remove the structure in the noise prior to
performing the multivariate analysis.
For the case of Poisson-distributed data, one approach
that is effective for eliminating structure in the noise ~i.e.,
making the noise covariance matrix look like the identity
matrix! is scaling the data by the inverse square root of the
mean spectrum as outlined earlier. Of course, this approach
will only be effective to the extent that the data are truly
Poisson. Figure 2 shows a histogram of the total counts per
voxel for the superalloy ROI of Figure 1c. It is important to
note that at this level of binning, only 0.29 counts per
spectrum are observed, on average. The distribution of total
counts predicted for a Poisson variable having a mean of
0.29 is also displayed in Figure 2 for comparison. The
agreement is very good. Figure 3 compares the eigenvalues

of the spectra contained in that volume. As noted in Keenan
~2007!, the eigenvalues corresponding to Poisson noise should
equal the summed spectrum. The fact that the sorted
summed spectrum agrees almost quantitatively with the
eigenvalues indicates, again, that PCA performed on the raw
data describes mostly noise. The inset in Figure 3 shows
part of the eigenvalue plot after the data have been scaled to
account for Poisson noise. The plot clearly suggests the
presence of a single significant component, as expected, and
the near equality of the eigenvalues corresponding to noise
indicates that the method has done a good job eliminating
structure from the noise.
One final point that should be mentioned is that APT
data follow the Poisson distribution only in the limit that
detecting any given atom in a single voxel is a rare event.
Figure 4 shows how the ratio of the variance in the total
number of atoms per voxel changes with the mean as APT
data are spatially binned more coarsely, that is, into bins of
larger size. For Poisson-distributed data, the variance should
equal the mean independently of the size of the voxel. For
APT data, however, the variance is a quadratic function of
the mean. Once the bins have reached a couple of nano-
meters on a side, the data deviate significantly from Poisson.
Interestingly, the actual behavior of APT data is exactly
opposite to the behavior supposed in Parish and Miller
~2010!. The origin of the quadratic behavior is understood,
and a fuller discussion of APT noise together with a method
for dealing sensibly with it during MVSA will be the subject
of a separate article.
Simulations
Simple simulations were carried out to investigate the feasi-
bility of using MVSA techniques to extract useful informa-
tion with atomic size scale resolution from APT data. These
calculations simulated the analysis of a pure nickel sample
using APT, and parameters included the structure and iso-
topic composition of pure nickel together with basic perfor-
mance characteristics of atom probe instruments such as
detection efficiency and spatial resolution. In the simplest
case, we considered sampling one million atom locations in
the nickel structure one site at a time. Given the detection
efficiency of the atom probe, taken to be 37% ~LEAP 3000X
HR, www.cameca.com!, there is a 63% probability that no
atom of any type will be found at a given location. The
remaining possibilities are that we detect a single atom and
that its identity is one of the five naturally occurring iso-
topes of nickel. A five-channel spectrum can be constructed
at each location by assigning one channel to each of the
possible isotopes and placinga1i nt h ec hannel correspond-
ing to the identity of the observed ion, and zeros in the
remaining channels. Note that 63% of the spectra will
contain nothing but zeros when we are looking at atom sites
one at a time. In the language of MVSA, the five-channel
spectrum is a multivariate observation, and the single latent
variable we are trying to identify is simply nickel. The
variable loadings for the nickel component, in this case, are
just the relative abundances of the respective isotopes.
Multivariate data having these characteristics—namely,
that each variable in the measurement is limited to taking
on a discrete set of possible values, each having a fixed
probability—are said to be multinomially distributed~Larsen
& Marx, 1986 !. The multinomial distribution is closely
related to the binomial distribution, and, in fact, each
individual variable examined separately is binomially distrib-
uted. The binomial distribution relates to measurements
that can only have two possible outcomes; for instance, an
individual atom of Ni is either
58Ni or it is not. For our
purposes, the most important property of the binomial
distribution is that in the limit of large numbers of observa-
tions of low probability events, the binomial distribution is
closely approximated by the Poisson distribution ~Larsen &
Marx, 1986 !. Thus, while a multinomial random number
generator was used to construct the simulations, the data
were analyzed assuming they are Poisson-distributed.
T able 1 lists the elements of the most significant
principal-component loading vector for the simulation when
the atoms are sampled one at a time. For both standard
PCA and Poisson-scaled PCA, only the loading correspond-
ing to
58Ni is nonzero. This was to be expected. Since two or
more atoms are never observed simultaneously, the data
appear uncorrelated ~D
T D is a diagonal matrix !, and since
the first principal component tries to account for the most
variance in the data, it identifies the most prevalent isotope
of nickel,
58Ni. The simple column scaling employed by the
Poisson-scaled PCA method cannot induce any correlations
among the isotopes, thus yields the same answer.
The situation becomes more interesting when the at-
oms are sampled two at a time. As shown in T able 1,
correlations among all of the isotopes are observed in the
first principal-component loading. The Poisson-scaled PCA
loading vector quantitatively describes the known relation-
ships among the isotope abundances. Thus, while at most
two different isotopes are observed in any single spectrum,

The foregoing discussion suggests that MVSA is feasible
when APT data are spatially binned to the size of two
atoms. In fact, the final simulation demonstrates that MVSA
can be applied successfully when APT data are binned to the
size of a single atom or smaller. The basic idea is that while
two atoms obviously cannot occupy the same physical loca-
tion, it can appear that more than one atom resides at the
same place when the data are binned. There are two reasons
for this. First, based on the fcc crystal structure of nickel
and its lattice parameter, 0.352 nm, it is simple to estimate
~Barrett & Massalski, 1980 ! that the diameter of a nickel
atom is approximately 0.25 nm. However, a cube whose
sides are only 0.176 nm long ~half the lattice parameter ! and
parallel to the sides of the unit cell, and centered on a
tetrahedral hole, will contain the centers of the four atoms
forming the tetrahedral hole at vertices of the cube. Thus,
any cube of this dimension or greater randomly placed on
the lattice will have nonzero probabilities of containing any
number up to four atoms. Second, the imperfect precision
with which the atom probe locates an atom can make it
appear that more than one atom resides at a given position.
The final simulation assumes that atoms in the nickel
lattice are located with a Gaussian root-mean-square preci-
sion of 0.2 nm in the lateral dimensions and 0.1 nm in
depth ~Gault et al., 2010 !. For this simulation, the atoms
were binned into cubes that are 0.2 nm on a side. The
simulated lattice was 76 /H1100376 /H1100352 nm in size ~37,544,000
voxels! and a total of 10,193,268 atoms were counted. Thus,
the probability of finding an atom of any type in any given
voxel was only 27%. Figure 5 displays a histogram of the
total number of atoms detected per bin. As expected, the
vast majority of the bins contain either zero or one count.
However, as many as six atoms are found in a single voxel,
and over 1 million voxels contain two or more atoms. While
this is only a few percent of the total number of voxels, it is
a large number in absolute terms and allows good statistical
estimates to be made. The histogram is compared with the
distribution expected from Poisson-distributed data, and
the agreement is very good as shown in Figure 5. W e also
note the close similarity of this histogram with the corre-
sponding plot made for the superalloy cube ROI and shown
in Figure 2. The first principal component of the lattice
simulation obtained by MVSA is included in T able 1, and
again, the Poisson-scaled PCA yields quantitatively correct
estimates of the nickel isotope distribution.

It is interesting to consider exactly how the MVSA
model should be interpreted when modeling the composi-
tion of a material at the atomic size scale. The native atom
probe model of a material predicts that a specific type of
atom is present at a particular spatial location. The multi-
variate model, on the other hand, only predicts that an
atom characteristic of a specific phase will be present there.
In the context of the nickel-lattice simulation, atom probe
analysis may find that a
58Ni atom resides at some particu-
lar site; the MSV A model will only predict that some isotope
of Ni will be present there, and that the likelihood of any
specific isotope residing at that location is given by its
relative natural abundance.
MVSA of the Nickel-Base Superalloy T wo-Phase
Region
Based on the total ion count for the nickel-base superalloy
two-phase ROI shown in Figure 1b, it is evident that there
are at least two different types of voxels having slightly
different total ion intensities. Thus, this sample should
provide a test of MVSA ’s ability to discover multiple compo-
nents in sparse APT data. The mean spectrum of the entire
ROI is shown in Figure 6. The mass spectrum appears
relatively simple, consisting largely of elements known to be
present from the bulk composition. A more revealing look
at the mean spectrum is obtained by replotting it on a
logarithmic scale as shown in Figure 7. Clearly, a wealth of
information is present in APT data; not immediately clear is
how to interpret it.
Eigenanalysis was performed on data spatially binned
to 0.2 nm cubes and scaled to account for Poisson statistics.
The eigenvalue plot in Figure 8 suggests the presence of
three significant components. Referring back to equation
~7!, eigenvalues corresponding to noise should be equal and
have a numerical value equal to the number of voxels if we
have properly dealt with the noise. A line corresponding to
the number of voxels is shown on the eigenvalue plot and,
on average, the noise eigenvalues correspond to the number
of voxels. Note that we do not expect exact equality. Since
we do not know the true noise covariance matrix, we must
estimate it from the data. The successive eigenvalues are
then weighted by the degrees of freedom associated with
their respective estimates ~Malinowski, 1987 !, which leads
to the slight slope the sorted eigenvalues exhibit in Figure 8.
The three most significant principal-component load-
ings obtained with the Poisson-scaled PCA method are
displayed in Figure 9. The first two loadings describe the
compositional similarities and differences among the major
constituents of the two-phase region. The third loading
describes an unexpected set of species that, as will be shown
below, are found at the interface. This component is describ-
ing two different things. First, there is an enhanced abun-
dance of singly charged metal ions relative to the doubly
charged ions predominant in PC1 and PC2 loadings, which
are associated with the bulk phases. Second, numerous
metal oxides are found that are not present in PC1 and PC2
loadings. T aken together, these three PCs explain most of
the unidentified mass peaks in the mean spectrum.

spectral characteristics of the various chemical phases, the
corresponding scores give information about their respec-

In the present case, it is likely that standard image
processing techniques could have been used to segment the
total ion image ~Fig. 10a ! into three ~or more ! distinct
groups of voxels, and each group of voxels could have been
studied individually. Of course, performing such processing
implies some foreknowledge about the sample that permits
an intelligent choice of segmentation criteria—specifically,
what masses are important to differentiating chemical phases
in the sample. This type of approach is prone to introduc-
ing bias into the results, and to missing small but potentially
important spectral features of which the analyst may not be
aware. Much of the power of MVSA resides in its ability to
use all of the collected information to very rapidly discover
the number of components, their spectral characteristics,
and spatial distributions in an automated way without
preconceived notions about what should be present and
where. Establishing the fact that all of the oxide species vary
together at the interface, for instance, would be tedious at
best using univariate ~manual! data analysis methods given
the very low levels of these species present in the sample.
MVSA can also find subtle variations in chemistry that
would be difficult to characterize otherwise ~Smentkowski
et al., 2009!. A comparison of the loadings in Figures 9a and

and tantalum present in the superalloy, and that they are
more prevalent in the Ni-rich phase than the Cr /Co-rich
phase. This may or may not be important to the perfor-
mance of the material; what is important is that this infor-
mation is made available to the analyst for further evaluation.
For a more complete discussion of automated analyses, the
power of MVSA and other topics addressed in this para-
graph, see Kotula et al. ~2003!.

the chemical information at the atomic size scale from large
APT datasets in a maximally parsimonious way. PCA was
our choice of multivariate method because it is guaranteed
that no p-component factor model can beat PCA in a
least-squares fitting sense, and because efficient algorithms
for computing PCA are commonly available. The drawback
of PCA is that because the constraints imposed on the

Computational Considerations
Atom probe datasets can be truly huge when spatially binned
to the size scale of a single atom. The dataset corresponding
to the complete nickel-base superalloy sample in Figure 1a
contains over 56 million detected ions. As noted earlier, the
probability of detecting any type of ion in any given voxel
with 0.2 nm sides is about 0.3; thus, we can infer that there
are almost 190 million voxels within the actual volume of
the sample, ignoring the void volume in the circumscribing
box. A double-precision, dense-matrix representation of
this dataset with a 200-channel mass spectrum at each voxel
would require about 280 Gbytes of computer storage. Clearly,
performing MVSA would pose a daunting challenge. Fortu-
nately, the raw APT data are presented to the user in a
sparse format, and it is possible, in fact essential, to main-
tain and exploit the sparsity throughout the entire analysis
process.
Parish and Miller ~2010! describe in detail the proce-
dure for mapping position-tagged ion masses contained in
the APT *.pos-file to indices in a four-dimensional ~4D!
array ~three spatial and one spectral !. The indices represent
the locations of the spatial and spectral bins having user-
defined sizes. They further describe how the 4D array is
unfolded into a matrix for linear algebra operations. W e will
not repeat that discussion here. Parish and Miller then
create a dense m /H11003n matrix and accumulate the observed
ion detections into the appropriate bins. An enormous
savings in storage can be accomplished by realizing that the
two d-vectors that contain the matrix row and column
indices, respectively, for each of the d detected ions, together
with a d-vector of ones reflecting the fact that each set of
indices tags a single ion, is simply the standard representa-
tion of a sparse matrix in triplet format ~Davis, 2006 !.I n
Matlab, the “sparse” command sums duplicate entries in the
triplet-formatted list and converts the matrix into so-called
compressed-column format, which is suitable for perform-
ing efficient sparse linear algebra operations. The storage
requirements for the compressed-column format are 2 /H11003d
plus the number of columns in the matrix. For reasons
related to how Matlab performs linear algebra, we actually
store the data matrix D as its transpose; thus, storage is

One final point concerns the explicit computation of
the principal-component scores in a way that is usable by
3D visualization tools. In the examples above, we computed
the nonzero scores and maintained a list of indices showing
where the nonzero scores were located. For display pur-
poses, it may be necessary to supply the full scores matrix,
including the locations where the scores are zero, to the
software. In this case, the storage requirements for the
scores may easily exceed that of the data matrix itself. This is
the significance of separating the task of computing the
loadings from the task of computing the scores in the
discussion leading up to equation ~17!.G i v e nas e to f
spectral basis vectors that span the spectral space of a raw
data matrix, the rotation matrix that transforms them into
principal-component loadings, and an estimate of the noise
covariance matrix, scores can be computed directly from
the raw data. One might envision, for instance, a process
that takes a stream of raw spectra as an input and outputs
the corresponding scores. An important point is that the
loadings do not need to be derived from the same dataset as
the scores, they only need to span the same space. Thus,
depending on the needs of the display software, scores at an
appropriate spatial resolution can be estimated from load-
ings obtained at a different resolution without having to
recompute the PCA factorization.
SUMMARY
W e have demonstrated for the first time the ability to
perform MVSA on APT data at the full spatial resolution of
the atom probe. Given that raw APT data consist of a list of
position-tagged single ion detections, it would at first seem
unlikely that the interelement correlations that MVSA ex-
ploits can be discovered on the size scale of a single atom.
However, while two atoms certainly cannot simultaneously
occupy the same physical location, the imperfect precision
with which the atom probe locates an atom can make it
appear that more than one atom resides at a given position.
That is, the single atoms that the atom probe detects can be
considered random samples drawn from a local neighbor-
hood defined by the spatial resolution the instrument. Viewed
this way, it would seem plausible that multivariate statistical
techniques could extract useful information from APT data
in an automated and unbiased manner. Specifically, MVSA
should be able to estimate the chemical compositions of

Using simple simulations, the principles outlined above
were demonstrated. Additionally, MVSA was successfully
applied to a real sample, a two-phase zone in a nickel-base
superalloy. Besides the chemical phases expected based on
the bulk composition of the superalloy, an unexpected
interface that is enriched in oxygenated species was
discovered.
Properly accounting for noise is critical to the success
of MVSA. V ariance-based MVSA methods, such as PCA,
attempt to account for variance in the dataset in a maxi-
mally parsimonious way, with no regard to whether that
variance arises from informative signals or random noise.
For APT data minimally binned to the atomic size scale, the
signal-to-noise ratio is very low. The most prevalent species
in the two-phase sample,
58Ni, is only found in about 5 out
of every 100 voxels, on average, when the data are binned
into cubes 0.2 nm on a side. Here we show that APT data
are approximately Poisson-distributed when binned to this
level; thus, the estimated standard deviation for the
58Ni
measurement is the square root of the mean, or about 0.22.
In other words, the signal-to-noise ratio is significantly less
than 1. Without properly accounting for the statistical na-
ture of the noise, MVSA will find only noise. A procedure
for dealing with Poisson-distributed data was developed
and shown to successfully discriminate chemical informa-
tion from noise in the case of real APT data aggregated at
the atomic scale. Interestingly, APT data binned more
coarsely, on the order of 1 nm or more, start to deviate
significantly from Poisson. Details of the noise characteris-
tics for coarsely binned APT data and a method to account
for this noise sensibly in a multivariate analysis will be the
subjects of a separate article.
It seems remarkable that MVSA can be successfully
applied to a spectral image when each spectrum contains
much less than one count, on average ~0.15 total counts per
voxel for the two-phase sample !. However, the paucity of
information in any single spectrum is more than counter-
balanced by the vast number of spectra, tens of millions or
more, in the dataset. Handling that number of spectra does
pose significant computational challenges. Over 50 million
200-channel mass spectra comprise the nickel-base superal-
loy two-phase ROI dataset. Simply to represent that dataset
by a double precision dense matrix would require over 75
Gbytes of memory. W e have presented a computational
approach that maintains the data in a sparse format from
generation through MVSA. Using these techniques, MVSA
at the atomic size scale can be accomplished in seconds on
computers with modest capabilities. Thus, the feasibility of
using MVSA as a routine tool for the analysis of APT data is
apparent.

Atomic-Scale Phase Composition through Multivariate
Statistical Analysis of Atom Probe Tomography Data
Michael R. Keenan, 1,* Vincent S. Smentkowski, 2 Robert M. Ulfig, 3 Edward Oltman, 3
David J. Larson, 3 and Thomas F . Kelly 3
18346 Roney Road, Wolcott, NY 14590, USA
2General Electric Global Research Center, Niskayuna, NY 12309, USA
3CAMECA Instruments, Inc., 5500 Nobel Drive, Madison, WI 53711, USA
Abstract: W e demonstrate for the first time that multivariate statistical analysis techniques can be applied to
atom probe tomography data to estimate the chemical composition of a sample at the full spatial resolution of
the atom probe in three dimensions. Whereas the raw atom probe data provide the specific identity of an
atom at a precise location, the multivariate results can be interpreted in terms of the probabilities that an atom
representing a particular chemical phase is situated there. When aggregated to the size scale of a single atom
~;0.2 nm!, atom probe spectral-image datasets are huge and extremely sparse. In fact, the average spectrum will
have somewhat less than one total count per spectrum due to imperfect detection efficiency. These conditions,
under which the variance in the data is completely dominated by counting noise, test the limits of multivariate
analysis, and an extensive discussion of how to extract the chemical information is presented. Efficient
numerical approaches to performing principal component analysis ~PCA! on these datasets, which may number
hundreds of millions of individual spectra, are put forward, and it is shown that PCA can be computed in a few
seconds on a typical laptop computer.
Key words: atom probe tomography ~APT!, multivariate statistical analysis ~MVSA!, PCA
INTRODUCTION
Multivariate statistical analysis ~MVSA! has proven to be an
effective approach for extracting the chemical information
from large spectral-image datasets in three spatial dimen-
sions. Examples include tomographic spectral imaging with
energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy ~EDXS!~ Kotula et al.,
2006!, and time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry
~TOF-SIMS!~ Smentkowski et al., 2007 !. In these cases,
a complete spectrum is acquired at each point, or voxel,
in a regular three-dimensional ~3D! array yielding an
abundance of data that can be cumbersome to analyze.
The assumption underlying MVSA is that a multivariate
observation—namely, an n-channel spectrum—is a linear
combination of a small number of hidden, or latent, vari-
ables. The goal of MVSA is to discover the latent variables,
which presumably describe the fundamental spectral char-
acteristics of the substances that make up the sample. If
there are p such latent variables with p
/H11021/H11021n,M V S Aa c c o m -
plishes a large reduction in the size and dimensionality of
the dataset. The presence of correlations among measured
variables is essential to the operation of MVSA. It is the
redundant information provided by correlated variables
that is discarded as the dataset is compressed by MVSA. For
a mass spectrometric detection scheme, correlated variables
might include, for example, multiple isotopes of a given
element or several elements that co-vary in a regular way as
in an alloy.
Implicit in the foregoing discussion is the notion that
several variables are measured simultaneously at each spa-
tial location, that is, that the observation is truly multivari-
ate. Atom probe tomography ~APT! data, on the other
hand, would seem to be fundamentally univariate in nature.
Since single atoms are detected one at a time, it is not
obvious how the interspecies correlations that MVSA ex-
ploits are captured in the data at the atomic level. Nonethe-
less, there has been recent interest in applying multivariate
techniques to APT data ~Keenan et al., 2010; Parish et al.,
2010; Parish & Miller, 2010 !. The common approach in the
cited works was to partition the sampled volume into a
large number of volume elements and to combine all of the
ions detected in each individual element into a single mass
spectrum. Typically, the volume elements were on the order
of 1 nm
3 and contained several to a few tens of detected
ions. Likewise, masses were binned to account for the fact
that the mass measurements have some imprecision. MVSA
was then performed on the aggregated data. Both groups
employed the same approach ~Keenan, 2009; Smentkowski
et al., 2009 ! whereby principal component analysis ~PCA!
was used to compress the data and extract the chemical
information, and the principal components were, option-
ally, post-processed to improve interpretability.
MVSA of APT data aggregated in this manner proved
successful, but at the cost of losing the high spatial resolu-
tion characteristic of atom probes. For a nominal lateral
resolution of 0.2 nm, binning to 1 nm
3 implies a factor of
125 loss of resolution on a volumetric basis. In this article
we will use simple arguments and simulations to suggest

that MVSA, in fact, can be successfully applied to APT data
aggregated to the native spatial resolution of the instru-
ment. Thus, we can gain the advantages of MVSA, namely,
discovering relationships among elements, while retaining
full spatial resolution. The arguments turn on the insight
that while the atom probe only detects single ions, those can
be considered random samples drawn from a local neigh-
borhood defined by the spatial resolution of the instru-
ment. When constructed at the atomic scale, the multivariate
model has a natural interpre tation. Rather than predicting
which specific atom is situated at a particular location, the
model predicts the probabilities that atoms of any type are
located there. In essence, the chemical-phase composition at
the atomic scale is resolved. These principles will be further
illustrated by analyzing an APT dataset obtained from a
two-phase region of a nickel-base superalloy sample using
PCA.
It should be noted that analyzing data binned to the
size of single atoms poses significant computational chal-
lenges. The number of voxels can easily reach tens to hun-
dreds of millions. Despite the high detection efficiency of
atom probes ~up to ;60%!, less than one ion will be
observed, on average, as arising from any single voxel.
Consequently, the matrices representing APT mass spectral
datasets are extremely sparse, a fact that must be exploited
to make the MVSA calculations tractable. Further efficien-
cies can be achieved by recalling that finding correlations
among variables is the central function of MVSA. Since the
observation of a single ion in isolation provides no informa-
tion about interatom correlations, we can restrict our atten-
tion to those mass spectra that contain two or more total
counts. This will typically be a tiny fraction of the total
number of spectra ~voxels!, yet still a large number in
absolute terms. With these considerations in mind, we will
discuss a computational approach using standard mathemat-
ical tools ~e.g., MATLAB, http://www.mathworks.com! that
enables the MVSA calculations to be performed on comput-
ers with modest capabilities. Using these techniques, PCA of
the superalloy dataset below was accomplished in a few
seconds on a standard laptop computer.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
The APT dataset employed in this work was obtained from
a sample of a nickel-base superalloy. Non-site-specific APT
specimens were prepared using the matchstick method and
electropolishing in 10% perchloric acid in acetic acid. Analy-
ses were obtained on a Cameca LEAP 3000X HR in voltage-
pulsed mode. This instrument has a detection efficiency of
37% of the ions independent of ion type. Atom positioning
precision is better than 1 nm in all locations and can be as
high as 0.06 nm in the longitudinal direction ~Z axis! of the
data ~Gault et al., 2010!.A no v e r v i e wo fa t o mp r o b et o m o g -
raphy can be found in Kelly and Miller ~2007!.
Figure 1a shows a 3D rendering of the total ion count
for this sample, which was obtained by binning the detected
ions into 1 nm
3 cubes. The ions included here were limited
to those having a mass-to-charge-state ratio of 100 Da or
less. The sample extends 171 nm in the axial dimension,
and at its maximum lateral extent, the sample has a diam-
eter of approximately 144 nm. A total of more than 55
million ions were detected. T wo regions of interest ~ROIs!
were selected for further study. A two-phase region shown
in Figure 1b was used to test the multivariate approach on a
sample having heterogeneous composition. This ROI com-
prises the top 39 nm of the sample, has a lateral extent of
100 nm at its base, and contains approximately 7.4 million
detected ions. For the multivariate analysis, a spectrum at
each voxel was constructed by binning all ions at a given
location into bins of 0.5 Da width centered on unit and
half-unit mass-to-charge-state ratio. The second ROI shown
in Figure 1c is a rectangular prism measuring 80 /H1100380 /H1100356
nm
3 and was used to evaluate the statistical properties of
APT data for a relatively homogeneous material. 12.5 mil-
lion detected ions were contained in this ROI.
Simple simulations that were performed were based on
the physical properties of pure nickel. The relevant proper-
ties include the relative abundances of the five naturally
occurring isotopes of pure nickel ~Rosman & T aylor, 1998!,
and the face-centered-cubic ~fcc! crystal structure of nickel
with a lattice parameter of 0.352 nm ~Barrett & Massalski,
1980!. It was assumed in all simulations that the probability
of detecting any given ion was 37%, regardless of the type of
ion or its location. Under these conditions, the data are
expected to follow the multinomial distribution ~Larsen &
Marx, 1986! because six possible outcomes ~an ion is one of
the five isotopes, or no ion is detected !, each with a known
probability of occurring, span the observation space when
looking at all atom positions one at a time. T o perform the
simulations, a simple multinomial random number gener-
ator was developed that was based on random numbers
uniformly distributed on the interval from zero to one.
Since the probabilities of the six events sum to one, all
possible outcomes can be mapped to nonoverlapping sub-
intervals on @01 #, where lengths of the subintervals are
equal to the respective probabilities of occurring. An “ob-

with each possessing a character istic spectrum, the spec-

spectra weighted by their respective abundances at that
location. In the absence of noise,

employed sparse-matrix operations. For a review of sparse-
matrix techniques with an emphasis on the MATLAB envi-
ronment, see Davis ~2006!. One operation, forming cross
products involving the data matrix, was implemented as a
C/H11001/H11001mex-file to prevent MATLAB from making unneces-
sary copies of large matrices. The computational times
reported here were measured on a Dell Latitude D630
laptop computer with 2 GB of memory running 32-bit
Windows XP .

spatially binned APT data. Since this volume contains an
essentially homogeneous material, it can be modeled in a
simple intuitive way without resorting to multivariate tech-
niques. Specifically, all voxels are assumed to have the same
underlying composition whose mass spectrum is given by
the mean spectrum over the entire dataset. In terms of the
linear model of equation ~1!, the noise-free data can be

One final point that should be mentioned is that APT
data follow the Poisson distribution only in the limit that
detecting any given atom in a single voxel is a rare event.
Figure 4 shows how the ratio of the variance in the total
number of atoms per voxel changes with the mean as APT
data are spatially binned more coarsely, that is, into bins of
larger size. For Poisson-distributed data, the variance should
equal the mean independently of the size of the voxel. For
APT data, however, the variance is a quadratic function of
the mean. Once the bins have reached a couple of nano-
meters on a side, the data deviate significantly from Poisson.
Interestingly, the actual behavior of APT data is exactly
opposite to the behavior supposed in Parish and Miller
~2010!. The origin of the quadratic behavior is understood,
and a fuller discussion of APT noise together with a method
for dealing sensibly with it during MVSA will be the subject
of a separate article.
Simulations
Simple simulations were carried out to investigate the feasi-
bility of using MVSA techniques to extract useful informa-
tion with atomic size scale resolution from APT data. These
calculations simulated the analysis of a pure nickel sample
using APT, and parameters included the structure and iso-
topic composition of pure nickel together with basic perfor-
mance characteristics of atom probe instruments such as
detection efficiency and spatial resolution. In the simplest
case, we considered sampling one million atom locations in
the nickel structure one site at a time. Given the detection
efficiency of the atom probe, taken to be 37% ~LEAP 3000X
HR, www.cameca.com!, there is a 63% probability that no
atom of any type will be found at a given location. The
remaining possibilities are that we detect a single atom and
that its identity is one of the five naturally occurring iso-
topes of nickel. A five-channel spectrum can be constructed
at each location by assigning one channel to each of the
possible isotopes and placinga1i nt h ec hannel correspond-
ing to the identity of the observed ion, and zeros in the
remaining channels. Note that 63% of the spectra will
contain nothing but zeros when we are looking at atom sites
one at a time. In the language of MVSA, the five-channel
spectrum is a multivariate observation, and the single latent
variable we are trying to identify is simply nickel. The
variable loadings for the nickel component, in this case, are
just the relative abundances of the respective isotopes.
Multivariate data having these characteristics—namely,
that each variable in the measurement is limited to taking
on a discrete set of possible values, each having a fixed
probability—are said to be multinomially distributed~Larsen
& Marx, 1986 !. The multinomial distribution is closely
related to the binomial distribution, and, in fact, each
individual variable examined separately is binomially distrib-
uted. The binomial distribution relates to measurements
that can only have two possible outcomes; for instance, an
individual atom of Ni is either
58Ni or it is not. For our
purposes, the most important property of the binomial
distribution is that in the limit of large numbers of observa-
tions of low probability events, the binomial distribution is
closely approximated by the Poisson distribution ~Larsen &
Marx, 1986 !. Thus, while a multinomial random number
generator was used to construct the simulations, the data
were analyzed assuming they are Poisson-distributed.
T able 1 lists the elements of the most significant
principal-component loading vector for the simulation when
the atoms are sampled one at a time. For both standard
PCA and Poisson-scaled PCA, only the loading correspond-
ing to
58Ni is nonzero. This was to be expected. Since two or
more atoms are never observed simultaneously, the data
appear uncorrelated ~D
T D is a diagonal matrix !, and since
the first principal component tries to account for the most
variance in the data, it identifies the most prevalent isotope
of nickel,
58Ni. The simple column scaling employed by the
Poisson-scaled PCA method cannot induce any correlations
among the isotopes, thus yields the same answer.
The situation becomes more interesting when the at-
oms are sampled two at a time. As shown in T able 1,
correlations among all of the isotopes are observed in the
first principal-component loading. The Poisson-scaled PCA
loading vector quantitatively describes the known relation-
ships among the isotope abundances. Thus, while at most
two different isotopes are observed in any single spectrum,

The foregoing discussion suggests that MVSA is feasible
when APT data are spatially binned to the size of two
atoms. In fact, the final simulation demonstrates that MVSA
can be applied successfully when APT data are binned to the
size of a single atom or smaller. The basic idea is that while
two atoms obviously cannot occupy the same physical loca-
tion, it can appear that more than one atom resides at the
same place when the data are binned. There are two reasons
for this. First, based on the fcc crystal structure of nickel
and its lattice parameter, 0.352 nm, it is simple to estimate
~Barrett & Massalski, 1980 ! that the diameter of a nickel
atom is approximately 0.25 nm. However, a cube whose
sides are only 0.176 nm long ~half the lattice parameter ! and
parallel to the sides of the unit cell, and centered on a
tetrahedral hole, will contain the centers of the four atoms
forming the tetrahedral hole at vertices of the cube. Thus,
any cube of this dimension or greater randomly placed on
the lattice will have nonzero probabilities of containing any
number up to four atoms. Second, the imperfect precision
with which the atom probe locates an atom can make it
appear that more than one atom resides at a given position.
The final simulation assumes that atoms in the nickel
lattice are located with a Gaussian root-mean-square preci-
sion of 0.2 nm in the lateral dimensions and 0.1 nm in
depth ~Gault et al., 2010 !. For this simulation, the atoms
were binned into cubes that are 0.2 nm on a side. The
simulated lattice was 76 /H1100376 /H1100352 nm in size ~37,544,000
voxels! and a total of 10,193,268 atoms were counted. Thus,
the probability of finding an atom of any type in any given
voxel was only 27%. Figure 5 displays a histogram of the
total number of atoms detected per bin. As expected, the
vast majority of the bins contain either zero or one count.
However, as many as six atoms are found in a single voxel,
and over 1 million voxels contain two or more atoms. While
this is only a few percent of the total number of voxels, it is
a large number in absolute terms and allows good statistical
estimates to be made. The histogram is compared with the
distribution expected from Poisson-distributed data, and
the agreement is very good as shown in Figure 5. W e also
note the close similarity of this histogram with the corre-
sponding plot made for the superalloy cube ROI and shown
in Figure 2. The first principal component of the lattice
simulation obtained by MVSA is included in T able 1, and
again, the Poisson-scaled PCA yields quantitatively correct
estimates of the nickel isotope distribution.

It is interesting to consider exactly how the MVSA
model should be interpreted when modeling the composi-
tion of a material at the atomic size scale. The native atom
probe model of a material predicts that a specific type of
atom is present at a particular spatial location. The multi-
variate model, on the other hand, only predicts that an
atom characteristic of a specific phase will be present there.
In the context of the nickel-lattice simulation, atom probe
analysis may find that a
58Ni atom resides at some particu-
lar site; the MSV A model will only predict that some isotope
of Ni will be present there, and that the likelihood of any
specific isotope residing at that location is given by its
relative natural abundance.
MVSA of the Nickel-Base Superalloy T wo-Phase
Region
Based on the total ion count for the nickel-base superalloy
two-phase ROI shown in Figure 1b, it is evident that there
are at least two different types of voxels having slightly
different total ion intensities. Thus, this sample should
provide a test of MVSA ’s ability to discover multiple compo-
nents in sparse APT data. The mean spectrum of the entire
ROI is shown in Figure 6. The mass spectrum appears
relatively simple, consisting largely of elements known to be
present from the bulk composition. A more revealing look
at the mean spectrum is obtained by replotting it on a
logarithmic scale as shown in Figure 7. Clearly, a wealth of
information is present in APT data; not immediately clear is
how to interpret it.
Eigenanalysis was performed on data spatially binned
to 0.2 nm cubes and scaled to account for Poisson statistics.
The eigenvalue plot in Figure 8 suggests the presence of
three significant components. Referring back to equation
~7!, eigenvalues corresponding to noise should be equal and
have a numerical value equal to the number of voxels if we
have properly dealt with the noise. A line corresponding to
the number of voxels is shown on the eigenvalue plot and,
on average, the noise eigenvalues correspond to the number
of voxels. Note that we do not expect exact equality. Since
we do not know the true noise covariance matrix, we must
estimate it from the data. The successive eigenvalues are
then weighted by the degrees of freedom associated with
their respective estimates ~Malinowski, 1987 !, which leads
to the slight slope the sorted eigenvalues exhibit in Figure 8.
The three most significant principal-component load-
ings obtained with the Poisson-scaled PCA method are
displayed in Figure 9. The first two loadings describe the
compositional similarities and differences among the major
constituents of the two-phase region. The third loading
describes an unexpected set of species that, as will be shown
below, are found at the interface. This component is describ-
ing two different things. First, there is an enhanced abun-
dance of singly charged metal ions relative to the doubly
charged ions predominant in PC1 and PC2 loadings, which
are associated with the bulk phases. Second, numerous
metal oxides are found that are not present in PC1 and PC2
loadings. T aken together, these three PCs explain most of
the unidentified mass peaks in the mean spectrum.

spectral characteristics of the various chemical phases, the

total ion image ~Fig. 10a ! into three ~or more ! distinct

implies some foreknowledge about the sample that permits

what masses are important to differentiating chemical phases
in the sample. This type of approach is prone to introduc-
ing bias into the results, and to missing small but potentially
important spectral features of which the analyst may not be
aware. Much of the power of MVSA resides in its ability to
use all of the collected information to very rapidly discover
the number of components, their spectral characteristics,
and spatial distributions in an automated way without
preconceived notions about what should be present and
where. Establishing the fact that all of the oxide species vary
together at the interface, for instance, would be tedious at
best using univariate ~manual! data analysis methods given
the very low levels of these species present in the sample.
MVSA can also find subtle variations in chemistry that
would be difficult to characterize otherwise ~Smentkowski
et al., 2009!. A comparison of the loadings in Figures 9a and

and tantalum present in the superalloy, and that they are
more prevalent in the Ni-rich phase than the Cr /Co-rich
phase. This may or may not be important to the perfor-
mance of the material; what is important is that this infor-
mation is made available to the analyst for further evaluation.
For a more complete discussion of automated analyses, the
power of MVSA and other topics addressed in this para-
graph, see Kotula et al. ~2003!.
This article has only been concerned with extracting
the chemical information at the atomic size scale from large
APT datasets in a maximally parsimonious way. PCA was
our choice of multivariate method because it is guaranteed
that no p-component factor model can beat PCA in a
least-squares fitting sense, and because efficient algorithms
for computing PCA are commonly available. The drawback
of PCA is that because the constraints imposed on the

Computational Considerations
Atom probe datasets can be truly huge when spatially binned
to the size scale of a single atom. The dataset corresponding
to the complete nickel-base superalloy sample in Figure 1a
contains over 56 million detected ions. As noted earlier, the
probability of detecting any type of ion in any given voxel
with 0.2 nm sides is about 0.3; thus, we can infer that there
are almost 190 million voxels within the actual volume of
the sample, ignoring the void volume in the circumscribing
box. A double-precision, dense-matrix representation of
this dataset with a 200-channel mass spectrum at each voxel
would require about 280 Gbytes of computer storage. Clearly,
performing MVSA would pose a daunting challenge. Fortu-
nately, the raw APT data are presented to the user in a
sparse format, and it is possible, in fact essential, to main-
tain and exploit the sparsity throughout the entire analysis
process.
Parish and Miller ~2010! describe in detail the proce-
dure for mapping position-tagged ion masses contained in
the APT *.pos-file to indices in a four-dimensional ~4D!
array ~three spatial and one spectral !. The indices represent
the locations of the spatial and spectral bins having user-
defined sizes. They further describe how the 4D array is
unfolded into a matrix for linear algebra operations. W e will
not repeat that discussion here. Parish and Miller then
create a dense m /H11003n matrix and accumulate the observed
ion detections into the appropriate bins. An enormous
savings in storage can be accomplished by realizing that the
two d-vectors that contain the matrix row and column
indices, respectively, for each of the d detected ions, together
with a d-vector of ones reflecting the fact that each set of
indices tags a single ion, is simply the standard representa-
tion of a sparse matrix in triplet format ~Davis, 2006 !.I n
Matlab, the “sparse” command sums duplicate entries in the
triplet-formatted list and converts the matrix into so-called
compressed-column format, which is suitable for perform-
ing efficient sparse linear algebra operations. The storage
requirements for the compressed-column format are 2 /H11003d
plus the number of columns in the matrix. For reasons
related to how Matlab performs linear algebra, we actually
store the data matrix D as its transpose; thus, storage is

which may be useful to the analyst when making mass
assignments. From a sparse matrix storage standpoint, the
width of a mass bin is irrelevant when only one or a few
ions are detected in each spectrum. Thus, we should be able
to bin the spectra to the native mass precision of the atom
probe with little effect on the memory requirements. That
this is indeed the case can be seen comparing columns ~c!
and ~d! in T able 2. The predominant cost of increased
spectral resolution is reflected in the time required to com-
pute the eigenanalysis of the data cross-product matrix, an
n /H11003n matrix. The time required to compute the complete
eigen-decomposition is expected to scale as n
3 ~Golub &
V an Loan, 1996!. This problem can be mitigated by comput-
ing only a few of the most significant eigenvalues and
eigenvectors. This is the approach taken by the AXSIA
spectral image analysis program ~Kotula et al., 2003! written
by one of the present authors ~M.R.K.!, where only the 64
most significant eigenvalues are considered. This same ap-
proach and number was also used by Parish and Miller
~2010!. The bottom two lines in T able 2 compare the PCA
computation times when either all or only 64 of the
eigenvalue/eigenvector pairs were computed. For relatively
low spectral resolution, there is no noticeable difference.

Sizable time savings can be realized with higher resolution
spectra, however. The high-resolution loading shown in
Figure 11d, aside from the improved resolution, appears
very similar to loadings obtained from the other subsets.
It is important to note that the multivariate analysis
results obtained from the ROI differ in no significant way
from those derived from the complete dataset. In the latter
case, no assumptions were made as to where the interesting
voxels were located or which masses provided the most
insight into the composition of the sample. All locations
and all masses were included in the analysis and treated
equivalently. The ability of MVSA to extract the chemical
information without the bias that might be introduced by
an analyst’s choice of ROI or channel selection is one of its
greatest strengths. Given that MVSA, in this case, exacts
virtually no cost suggests that it should become an impor-
tant element of the analyst’s toolbox.
One final point concerns the explicit computation of
the principal-component scores in a way that is usable by
3D visualization tools. In the examples above, we computed
the nonzero scores and maintained a list of indices showing
where the nonzero scores were located. For display pur-
poses, it may be necessary to supply the full scores matrix,
including the locations where the scores are zero, to the
software. In this case, the storage requirements for the
scores may easily exceed that of the data matrix itself. This is
the significance of separating the task of computing the
loadings from the task of computing the scores in the
discussion leading up to equation ~17!.G i v e nas e to f
spectral basis vectors that span the spectral space of a raw
data matrix, the rotation matrix that transforms them into
principal-component loadings, and an estimate of the noise
covariance matrix, scores can be computed directly from
the raw data. One might envision, for instance, a process
that takes a stream of raw spectra as an input and outputs
the corresponding scores. An important point is that the
loadings do not need to be derived from the same dataset as
the scores, they only need to span the same space. Thus,
depending on the needs of the display software, scores at an
appropriate spatial resolution can be estimated from load-
ings obtained at a different resolution without having to
recompute the PCA factorization.
SUMMARY
W e have demonstrated for the first time the ability to
perform MVSA on APT data at the full spatial resolution of
the atom probe. Given that raw APT data consist of a list of
position-tagged single ion detections, it would at first seem
unlikely that the interelement correlations that MVSA ex-
ploits can be discovered on the size scale of a single atom.
However, while two atoms certainly cannot simultaneously
occupy the same physical location, the imperfect precision
with which the atom probe locates an atom can make it
appear that more than one atom resides at a given position.
That is, the single atoms that the atom probe detects can be
considered random samples drawn from a local neighbor-
hood defined by the spatial resolution the instrument. Viewed
this way, it would seem plausible that multivariate statistical
techniques could extract useful information from APT data
in an automated and unbiased manner. Specifically, MVSA
should be able to estimate the chemical compositions of

neighborhoods on the atomic size scale without precon-

Using simple simulations, the principles outlined above
were demonstrated. Additionally, MVSA was successfully
applied to a real sample, a two-phase zone in a nickel-base
superalloy. Besides the chemical phases expected based on
the bulk composition of the superalloy, an unexpected
interface that is enriched in oxygenated species was
discovered.
Properly accounting for noise is critical to the success
of MVSA. V ariance-based MVSA methods, such as PCA,
attempt to account for variance in the dataset in a maxi-
mally parsimonious way, with no regard to whether that
variance arises from informative signals or random noise.
For APT data minimally binned to the atomic size scale, the
signal-to-noise ratio is very low. The most prevalent species
in the two-phase sample,
58Ni, is only found in about 5 out
of every 100 voxels, on average, when the data are binned
into cubes 0.2 nm on a side. Here we show that APT data
are approximately Poisson-distributed when binned to this
level; thus, the estimated standard deviation for the
58Ni
measurement is the square root of the mean, or about 0.22.
In other words, the signal-to-noise ratio is significantly less
than 1. Without properly accounting for the statistical na-
ture of the noise, MVSA will find only noise. A procedure
for dealing with Poisson-distributed data was developed
and shown to successfully discriminate chemical informa-
tion from noise in the case of real APT data aggregated at
the atomic scale. Interestingly, APT data binned more
coarsely, on the order of 1 nm or more, start to deviate
significantly from Poisson. Details of the noise characteris-
tics for coarsely binned APT data and a method to account
for this noise sensibly in a multivariate analysis will be the
subjects of a separate article.
It seems remarkable that MVSA can be successfully
applied to a spectral image when each spectrum contains
much less than one count, on average ~0.15 total counts per
voxel for the two-phase sample !. However, the paucity of
information in any single spectrum is more than counter-
balanced by the vast number of spectra, tens of millions or
more, in the dataset. Handling that number of spectra does
pose significant computational challenges. Over 50 million
200-channel mass spectra comprise the nickel-base superal-
loy two-phase ROI dataset. Simply to represent that dataset
by a double precision dense matrix would require over 75
Gbytes of memory. W e have presented a computational
approach that maintains the data in a sparse format from
generation through MVSA. Using these techniques, MVSA
at the atomic size scale can be accomplished in seconds on
computers with modest capabilities. Thus, the feasibility of
using MVSA as a routine tool for the analysis of APT data is
apparent.
